standard_description: Solve word problems involving addition and subtraction of fractions referring to the same whole, including cases of unlike denominators, e.g., by using visual fraction models or equations to represent the problem. Use benchmark fractions and number sense of fractions to estimate mentally and assess the reasonableness of answers. For example, recognize an incorrect result 2/5 + 1/2 = 3/7, by observing that 3/7 < 1/2.
subject: Math

Problem 2
Jamal is completing a math problem and represents the expression
$${-5{5\over7}+8-3{2\over7}}$$
with a single rational number as shown in the steps below. Explain what Jamal did in each step and check his work along the way.
$${=-5{5\over7}+8+ \left (-3{2\over7} \right)}$$
$${=-5{5\over7} +\left (-3{2\over7} \right) + 8}$$
$${=-5 + \left( -{5\over7} \right) +(-3)+ \left (-{2\over7} \right) +8}$$
$${=-5 + \left( -{5\over7} \right) + \left (-{2\over7} \right) + (-3) +8}$$
$${= -5 + (-1) + (-3) + 8}$$
$${= -6 + (-3) + 8}$$
$${=(-9)+8}$$
$${=-1}$$

5.NF Salad Dressing
Alignments to Content Standards:  5.NF.A.2 5.NF.B.7.c
Task
Aunt Barb’s Salad Dressing Recipe
 
 cup olive oil
 cup balsamic vinegar
a pinch of herbs
a pinch of salt
Makes 6 servings
a. How many cups of salad dressing will this recipe make? Write an equation to
represent your thinking. Assume that the herbs and salt do not change the amount of
dressing.
b. If this recipe makes 6 servings, how much dressing would there be in one serving?
Write a number sentence to represent your thinking.
IM Commentary
The purpose of this task is to have students add fractions with unlike denominators
and divide a unit fraction by a whole number. This accessible real-life context provides
students with an opportunity to apply their understanding of addition as joining two
separate quantities. Additionally, the context presents a "how many groups" division
problem where a unit fraction should be divided into 6 equal groups.

The Standards for Mathematical Practice focus on the nature of the learning
experiences by attending to the thinking processes and habits of mind that students
need to develop in order to attain a deep and flexible understanding of mathematics.
Certain tasks lend themselves to the demonstration of specific practices by students.
The practices that are observable during exploration of a task depend on how
instruction unfolds in the classroom. While it is possible that tasks may be connected to
several practices, only one practice connection will be discussed in depth. Possible
secondary practice connections may be discussed but not in the same degree of detail. 
This particular task helps illustrate Mathematical Practice Standard 4, Model with
mathematics. Students apply the mathematics they know to solve problems arising in
everyday life. For this problem, fifth graders apply their understanding of addition as
joining two separate quantities along with their understanding of division of a unit
fraction by a whole number. The addition of fractions with unlike denominators can be
shown symbolically or by creating a bar diagram depicting 1/3 cup of olive oil and 1/6
cup of balsamic vinegar. In order to make sense of these quantities, students will
subdivide the 1/3 cup of olive oil into sixths. Then they can visually see that the two
ingredients total 3/6 of a cup which is ½ of one whole cup.  This same method may be
used to divide the ½ cup of salad dressing into 6 equal portions. In each instance,
students use the mathematics they know to find a solution pathway and then capture
their thinking in an equation.  By using the mathematical model of an equation to
represent the real life situation described in the task students exercise their ability to
model with the mathematics that they know.

khan_exercises.title: Identify unit fractions
khan_exercises.description: Identify unit fractions when given a visual or a context.

items.question_content:
The sun hits $1$ of the $4$ equal-sized sections of an ice skating rink more than the others.  This section is extra slippery because the sun has melted it some.

[[☃ image 1]]

 
**What fraction of the ice skating rink is extra slippery because of the sun?**  

[[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints: There are $4$ sections, and they are all the same size. The total number of equal parts is $4$.
When we have $4$ equal parts, then the area of each part is $\dfrac{1}{4}$ of the total area.
Since the sun hits $1$ section more than the others, we say that  $\dfrac{1}{4}$ , or one-fourth, of the ice skating rink is extra slippery.

options.alt: A circle divided into four equal parts with one part shaded.

items.question_content:
Scarlett cuts the grass in her grandparents' yard.  Her grandparents' yard is made up of $2$ equal-sized sections.  So far, Scarlett has cut the grass in $1$ section.     

[[☃ image 1]]

 
**What fraction of the grass has Scarlett cut?**   

[[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints: There are $2$ sections, and they are all the same size. The total number of equal parts is $2$.
When we have $2$ equal parts, then the area of each part is $\dfrac{1}{2}$ of the total area.
Since Scarlett has only cut $1$ section, we say that she has cut $\dfrac{1}{2}$ , or one-half, of the grass.

options.alt: A square divided into two equal parts with one part shaded.

items.question_content:
Denis baked a peach pie and cut it into $3$ equal-sized pieces.  Denis's dad eats $1$ section of the pie.  

[[☃ image 1]]

**What fraction of the pie did Denis's dad eat?**  

[[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints: There are $3$ pieces, and they are all the same size. The total number of equal parts is $3$.
When we have $3$ equal parts, then the area of each part is $\dfrac{1}{3}$ of the total area.
Since Denis's dad only ate $1$ piece, we say that  Denis's dad ate $\dfrac{1}{3}$ , or one-third, of the peach pie.

options.alt: A circle divided into three equal parts with one part shaded.

items.question_content:

hints: There are $3$ sections, and they are all the same size. The total number of equal parts is $3$.

Since the broccoli is only in $1$ section, we say that  $\dfrac{1}{3}$ , or one-third, of the plate has broccoli.

options.alt: A circle divided into three equal parts with one part shaded.

options.alt: A rectangle divided into three equal parts with one part shaded.

items.question_content:

Since only $1$ section allows soccer, we say that $\dfrac{1}{3}$ , or one-third, of the blacktop is available to soccer players.

options.alt: A rectangle divided into three equal parts with one part shaded.

khan_exercises.description: Practice decomposing fractions (breaking fractions into parts that can be added together).
topic_path: Module 3: Addition and subtractions of fractions

items.question_content:
**Which of these sums equal $\dfrac{6}{3}$ ?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
First, let's visualize $\dfrac{6}{3}$.

[[☃ image 1]]

Now, let's compare each answer choice to $\dfrac{6}{3}$.

[[☃ image 1]]

[[☃ image 2]]

$\greenD{\dfrac{4}{2}} + \purpleD{\dfrac{2}{1}}  \neq {\dfrac{6}{3}}$

[[☃ image 1]]

[[☃ image 2]]

$\blueD{\dfrac{5}{3}} + \greenD{\dfrac{1}{3}} + \purpleD{\dfrac{2}{3}} + \red{\dfrac{1}{3}}  \neq {\dfrac{6}{3}}$

[[☃ image 1]]

[[☃ image 2]]

$\blueD{\dfrac{2}{3}} + \greenD{\dfrac{2}{3}} + \purpleD{\dfrac{2}{3}} \overset{\checkmark}=  {\dfrac{6}{3}}$

The following sum equals ${\dfrac{6}{3}}$:

* $\dfrac{2}{3} + \dfrac{2}{3} + \dfrac{2}{3}$

items.question_content:
**Which fractions add together to make $\dfrac{13}{2}$ ?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: All of the fractions have the same denominator, $2$, so the parts have the same size. We are ready to add.

The numerators tell us how many parts we are counting. We want $13$ equal parts.

$\blueD{1}+\greenD{4}+\purpleD{8}=13$



[[☃ image 1]]

$\blueD{\dfrac{1}{2}}+\greenD{\dfrac{4}{2}} +\purpleD{\dfrac{8}{2}} =\dfrac{13}{2}$

items.question_content:
**Compare with $>,<,$ or $=$.**


$2.09$ [[☃ dropdown 1]] $1$ one and $19$ tenths
hints:
Let's compare $\pink{2.09}$ and $\blueD{1\text{ one and } 19 \text{ tenths}}$ with visuals.  
*(Each big square represents one whole.)*

$\pink{2.09}:$|![A big square is completely shaded pink.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/cd34526d153208c0fb2c5884b5ca657d51e1c147) ![A big square is completely shaded pink.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/cd34526d153208c0fb2c5884b5ca657d51e1c147) ![A big square divided into 100 equal sized squares that are set in 10 rows of 10 squares each. In the bottom row, the left 9 squares are shaded pink.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/acd180541221eb345f85188137c88cfbae894a27)
- | - | -
$\blueD{1\text{ one and } 19 \text{ tenths}}:$ | ![A big square is completely shaded blue.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/2608fd9f2206158155e1878d7011dff5ce40b91a) ![A big square divided into 10 equal sized rectangles that are set in 10 rows of 1 rectangle each. All 10 rows with rectangles are shaded blue.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/56cdf4ffa6b3592914737fdd64959ccf85f99c6f) ![A big square divided into 10 equal sized rectangles that are set in 10 rows of 1 rectangle each. 9 bottom rows with rectangles are shaded blue.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/3a6746820919f4d8f3490a0b69e768be177902be)
$\pink{2.09}$ covers **less** area than $\blue{2.9}$, so $\pink{2.09}$ is **less** than $\blue{2.9}$.
$\pink{2.09}< \blue{1 \text{ one and }19 \text{ tenths}}$

Numbers are equivalent when they represent the same place on the number line.

[[☃ image 1]]

$\greenD{\dfrac14} \neq \blueD{\dfrac26}$

$\purpleD{N}$ is located at $\purpleD{\dfrac48}$.



[[☃ image 1]]

$\purpleD{\dfrac48} \neq \blueD{\dfrac26}$

$\maroonD{L}$ is located at $\maroonD{\dfrac13}$.



[[☃ image 1]]

$\maroonD{\dfrac13} = \blueD{\dfrac26}$
$\maroonD{L}$ is in the same place on the number line as $\blueD{\dfrac26}$.

The following number line is equivalent to $\dfrac{2}{6}$.



[[☃ image 1]]
